title: Joins
text:
QUESTION 1
Write an SQL query that pulls in the the salaries table and the collegeplaying table (and any other tables you might need) and store them in polars dataframes. In Python Polars, combine the dataframes to create a list of baseball players who attended BYU-Idaho. The new table should contain five columns: playerID, schoolID, salary, yearID and teamID associated with each salary.
Multiple players from BYU-Idaho were successfully trained and played at a professional level. The data spans several decades, with salaries ranging from $150k (Matt Lindstrom and Garrett Stephenson in their early years) to as high as $4M in later seasons. Players also were on multiple big teams, including the Phillies, Cardinals, Orioles, and White Sox.


Show the code
salaries = pl.read_database("SELECT * FROM salaries", con)
college_playing = pl.read_database("SELECT * FROM collegeplaying", con)

byui_players = (
    salaries
    .join(college_playing, on="playerID", how="inner")
    .filter(pl.col("schoolID").str.to_lowercase().str.strip_chars() == "idbyuid") 
    .select(["playerID", "schoolID", "salary", "yearID", "teamID"])
    .unique()
)

print(byui_players)

QUESTION 2

Pick any two baseball teams and compare them using a metric of your choice (average salary, home runs, number of wins, etc). Be creative! Write an SQL query to read in the table(s) you need, then use polars to manipulate the data. Finally, make a graph using Lets-Plot to visualize the comparison. What do you learn?

I compared the Yankees and Red Sox, and found that both teams maintained relatively low and similar payrolls through the 80s and 90s, but around 1995, both teams starting rapidly spending more and more each season. While both teams increased, the Yankees tended to have a higher payroll overall. The Yankees were also the first team in the dataset to break $200M in annual payroll.


Show the code
q_compare = "SELECT teamID, yearID, salary FROM salaries WHERE teamID IN ('NYA', 'BOS')"
df_compare = pl.read_database(q_compare, con)

payroll_history = (
    df_compare
    .group_by(["teamID", "yearID"])
    .agg(pl.col("salary").sum().alias("total_payroll"))
    .sort("yearID")
)

p = (ggplot(payroll_history, aes(x='yearID', y='total_payroll', color='teamID')) 
     + geom_line(size=1.5)
     + geom_point()
     + scale_y_continuous(format='$,.0f')
     + labs(title="Historical Payroll Comparison: Yankees vs. Red Sox",
            subtitle="Comparing total annual salaries (1985-Present)",
            x="Year", y="Total Payroll (USD)",
            color="Team")
     + theme_minimal()
     + ggsize(800, 500)
     + theme(
         legend_position='bottom',
         plot_title=element_text(size=18), 
         plot_subtitle=element_text(size=12)
     )
)

p.show()

title: Baseball Database
text:
QUESTION 1
Use the provided code above to read in the batting table. Then, with Python Polars manipulate the вҖҳresultsвҖҷ dataframe to calculate the number of at bats and the batting average for each year a player played. Filter the data frame to only include players with at least 10 at bats that year. Sort the dataframe from highest batting average to lowest, and then by playerid alphabetically. Show the top 5 results in your report.
Note: A batting average is meant to measure how often a player puts the ball in play and gets to base safely. For this task, use a simplified version of batting average defined as the sum of hits (labeled вҖҳHвҖҷ) divided by number of at bats (labeled вҖҳABвҖҷ).
By filtering by a minimum of only 10 at-bats, the top results show players who may have technically had the highest batting averages like Nyls Nyman, but may not be the most representative of a playerвҖҷs overall performance (they may have played only a few games in that season).


Show the code
# Calculate seasonal stats
q1_results = (
    results
    .select(["playerID", "yearID", "H", "AB"])
    # Group by player and year to handle cases where players moved teams mid-season
    .group_by(["playerID", "yearID"])
    .agg([
        pl.col("H").sum(),
        pl.col("AB").sum()
    ])
    # Filter for at least 10 at bats
    .filter(pl.col("AB") >= 10)
    # Calculate Batting Average (BA)
    .with_columns(
        (pl.col("H") / pl.col("AB")).alias("batting_avg")
    )
    # Sort: BA descending, then playerID ascending
    .sort(["batting_avg", "playerID"], descending=[True, False])
    .head(5)
)

print(q1_results)

title: Baseball Database
text:
QUESTION 2
Use the provided code above to read in the batting table. Then, with Python Polars manipulate the вҖҳresultsвҖҷ dataframe to calculate the batting average for each player over their entire career (all years combined). Only include players with at least 100 at bats over their entire career. Sort the dataframe from highest batting average to lowest, and then by playerid alphabetically. Show the top 5 results in your report.
Note: A batting average is meant to measure how often a player puts the ball in play and gets to base safely. For this task, use a simplified version of batting average defined as the sum of hits (labeled вҖҳHвҖҷ) divided by number of at bats (labeled вҖҳABвҖҷ).
If we raise the threshold to 100 at-bats, the leaderboard quickly excludes any вҖңone hit wondersвҖқ from the list and starts to show us the truly greatest players. Ty Cobb is at the top of the list with a .366 career average, which is the MLB record for players with significant career longevity.


Show the code
# Calculate career stats
q2_results = (
    results
    .select(["playerID", "H", "AB"])
    # Group by player only to combine all years
    .group_by("playerID")
    .agg([
        pl.col("H").sum().alias("career_H"),
        pl.col("AB").sum().alias("career_AB")
    ])
    # Filter for at least 100 career at bats
    .filter(pl.col("career_AB") >= 100)
    # Calculate Career Batting Average
    .with_columns(
        (pl.col("career_H") / pl.col("career_AB")).alias("career_ba")
    )
    # Sort: BA descending, then playerID ascending
    .sort(["career_ba", "playerID"], descending=[True, False])
    .head(5)
)

print(q2_results)

title: Show me!
text:
QUESTION 1

Repeat the classification exercise using 2 different algorithms/models. Explain the differences between the 3 models (your original one, and the 2 new ones for this task).

I compared a Decision Tree, Random Forest, and Gradient Boosting model. The Decision Tree is the simplest baseline and decides things using a single flowchart type of logic, it had the lowest overall accuracy at 90%. The Random Forest improves on this by making a вҖңforestвҖқ of decision trees that vote on the final prediciton, reaching 92% accuracy. Gradient Boosting Models build trees sequentially, with each tree trying to correct the errors of the previous ones, often leading to improved performance (almost 93% accuracy in this case).


Show the code
# Include and execute your code here
dwellings = dwellings.with_columns(
    before1980 = pl.when(pl.col("yrbuilt") < 1980).then(1).otherwise(0)
)

X = dwellings.drop(["before1980", "before_1980", "yrbuilt", "parcel"], strict=False)
y = dwellings["before1980"]

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X.to_pandas(), y.to_pandas(), test_size=0.20, random_state=42
)


models = {
    "Decision Tree": DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=10, random_state=42),
    "Random Forest": RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, max_depth=15, random_state=42),
    "Gradient Boosting": GradientBoostingClassifier(n_estimators=100, learning_rate=0.1, max_depth=5, random_state=42)
}


for name, model in models.items():
    model.fit(X_train, y_train)
    y_pred = model.predict(X_test)
    acc = metrics.accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)
    print(f"{name} Accuracy: {acc:.2%}")

For each model (your original one, and the 2 new ones for this task), display the classification report and the permutation importances for the most important features. Discuss which model you would recommend to the Client and why.

The Gradient Boosting model performed the best with the highest accuracy (93%), followed by the Random Forest and then the Decision Tree. I would recommend the Gradient Boosting model to the Client because it provides the most accurate predictions for identifying pre-1980 versus post-1980 housing styles.


Show the code
# Include and execute your code here
import polars.selectors as cs

def get_importance_plot(model, name):
    result = permutation_importance(model, X_test.iloc[:500], y_test.iloc[:500], n_repeats=5, random_state=42)
    
    perm_df = pd.DataFrame({
        'feature': X_test.columns,
        'importance': result.importances_mean
    }).sort_values('importance', ascending=False).head(10)
    
    return ggplot(perm_df, aes(x='importance', y='feature', fill='importance')) + \
           geom_bar(stat='identity') + \
           labs(title=f"Permutation Importance: {name}")


for name, model in models.items():
    print(f"\n" + "="*30)
    print(f"REPORT FOR: {name}")
    print("="*30)
    
    y_pred = model.predict(X_test)
    print(metrics.classification_report(y_test, y_pred))

    p = get_importance_plot(model, name)
    p.show()

title: Can You Predict That?
text:
QUESTION

Take time to get to know your data. Based on some initial exploration, identify 3 variables in the dataset you think will have strong predictive ability for determining if a house was built before 1980. Explain/justify the reasoning behind your guesses (for example with plots).

The three most effective variables I found to use for analysis in my model were one story, living area, and number of bathrooms. I created a few plots to see the differences, and it showed a large concentration of ranch homes built before 1980, and much fewer after that. The living area plot also showed a big increase in living area for homes built after 1980. Older homes also had an average of 2 bathrooms, whereas more modern ones had around 3. Any price category is also a dead giveaway for the year built due to the substantial difference in prices over time, and these categories are what helped me push my model in q2 over 90%.


Show the code
import polars.selectors as cs

analysis = (
    dwellings
    .select(cs.numeric())
    .group_by("before1980")
    .agg(pl.all().mean())
    .transpose(include_header=True, header_name="variable", column_names=["Post_1980", "Pre_1980"])
)

analysis = analysis.with_columns(
    diff = (pl.col("Pre_1980") - pl.col("Post_1980")).abs()
).sort("diff", descending=True)

print("Top Variables by Mean Difference:")
print(analysis.head(10))

df_plot = dwellings.to_pandas()

p2 = ggplot(df_plot, aes(x='yrbuilt', fill='one_story_str')) + \

    geom_vline(xintercept=1980, linetype='dashed', color='red') + \
    labs(title = "One-Story Homes Over Time", fill = "Is One-Story?")

I used a Random Forest classifier model that reached a 92.86% accuracy. The data was prepared by removing the year built and parcel columns to prevent data leakage, and then used an 80/20 train-test split. Increasing the number of estimators and adjusting the max depth improved the performance, as well as a balaced class weight to account for any distribution imbalances between pre and post 1980 homes.

X = dwellings.drop(["before1980", "before_1980", "yrbuilt", "parcel"], strict=False)

    y.to_pandas(), 
    test_size=0.20, 
    random_state=42
)

    max_depth=20, 
    min_samples_split=5,
    random_state=42,
    class_weight='balanced' 
)

y_pred = rf.predict(X_test)

title: Missing values
text:
QUESTION 1

Convert values to missing Start with a fresh copy of the flights dataset by reading it in from the link provided above (as opposed to starting with the dataset you ended with in the last assignment). Identify data values that are problematic, non-sense or impossible and change them to be missing. In your report include one record example (one row) from your clean data, in the raw JSON format. Your example should display at least one missing value so that we can verify it was done correctly. (Note: JSON will convert NaNвҖҷs to null). Describe your process for finding values that needed to be changed, and how you changed them.

There were several problematic values in the dataset that needed to be converted to missing values. These included вҖң-999вҖқ, вҖң1500+вҖқ, and вҖңn/aвҖқ. The code below identifies these values and converts them to null (None) in the dataset to make calculating the mean and median values more accurate.


Show the code
import json

bad_values = ["-999", "1500+", "n/a"]

df_clean = df.with_columns([
    pl.when(pl.col(name).cast(pl.Utf8).is_in(bad_values))
    .then(None)
    .otherwise(pl.col(name))
    .alias(name) 
    for name in df.columns
])

example_row = df_clean.filter(pl.col("num_of_delays_total").is_null()).head(1)

print(json.dumps(example_row.to_dicts(), indent=4))


[]

title: Missing values
text:
QUESTION 2

Filling in missing data For all the вҖңminutes_delayedвҖқ columns, fill in any missing values with the median of that column. For the вҖңnum_ofвҖқ columns, fill in missing values with that columnвҖҷs mean.

Nothing to write/report for this question


Show the code
df_filled = df_clean.with_columns([
    pl.col("^minutes_delayed.*$").cast(pl.Float64).fill_null(pl.col("^minutes_delayed.*$").cast(pl.Float64).median()),
    pl.col("^num_of.*$").cast(pl.Float64).fill_null(pl.col("^num_of.*$").cast(pl.Float64).mean())
])

print(f"Mean num_of_delays_carrier: {df_filled['num_of_delays_carrier'].mean():.2f}")
print(f"Mean minutes_delayed_nas: {df_filled['minutes_delayed_nas'].mean():.2f}")

title: Missing values

QUESTION 3

Display a histogram for num_of_delays_carrier and for minutes_delayed_nas (2 separate histograms in total). Comment on the shape of each one. Also report the mean of each of these columns now that missing values have been filled in.

The histograms show that the distribution of carrier delays is multimodal and right-skewed, with most flights having a small number of delays. There is also a spike around the 700 mark, which corresponds with where we filled the missing values with the mean. title: Creating New Columns
text:
QUESTION 1

Create a new вҖҳseasonвҖҷ column. Define вҖҳseasonвҖҷ as:

Winter for January, Febuary (note the mispelling), March
Spring for April, May, June
Summer for July, August, September
Fall for October, November, December
All other rows can be assigned a value of вҖҳUnknownвҖҷ


No discussion needed for this question


Show the code
# Create the 'season' column using pl.when().then().otherwise()
df = df.with_columns(
    pl.when(pl.col("month").is_in(["January", "Febuary", "March"]))
    .then(pl.lit("Winter"))
    .when(pl.col("month").is_in(["April", "May", "June"]))
    .then(pl.lit("Spring"))
    .when(pl.col("month").is_in(["July", "August", "September"]))
    .then(pl.lit("Summer"))
    .when(pl.col("month").is_in(["October", "November", "December"]))
    .then(pl.lit("Fall"))
    .otherwise(pl.lit("Unknown"))
    .alias("season")
)

title: Creating New Columns
text:
QUESTION 2

Divide all the columns by 1000 whose name starts with num_of. Be sure to first filter out rows that contain problematic values for the columns like -999 or 1500+.

No discussion needed for this question

df = df.filter(
    ~pl.any_horizontal(
        pl.col("^num_of.*$").cast(pl.Utf8).is_in(["-999", "1500+"])
    )
)

df = df.with_columns(
    (pl.col("^num_of.*$").cast(pl.Float64) / 1000)
)

title: Creating New Columns
text:
QUESTION 3

According to the BTS website, the вҖңWeatherвҖқ category only accounts for severe weather delays. Mild weather delays are not counted in the вҖңWeatherвҖқ category, but are actually included in both the вҖңnasвҖқ and вҖңlate_aircraftвҖқ categories. Using the columns you modified in the bullet above, your job is to create a new column that calculates the total number of flights (in 1,000вҖҷs) delayed by weather (both severe and mild). Show your work by printing the first 5 rows of the dataset. Use these three rules for your calculations:

100% of delayed flights in the Weather category are due to weather

30% of all delayed flights in the вҖңlate_aircraftвҖқ category are due to weather

From April through August, 40% of delayed flights in the NAS category are due to weather. The rest of the months, the proportion rises to 65%


No discussion needed for this question


Show the code
# Calculate total weather delays (severe + mild) based on the three rules
df = df.with_columns(
    total_weather_delay = (
        pl.col("num_of_delays_weather") + 
        (0.30 * pl.col("num_of_delays_late_aircraft")) + 
        pl.when(pl.col("month").is_in(["April", "May", "June", "July", "August"]))
        .then(0.40 * pl.col("num_of_delays_nas"))
        .otherwise(0.65 * pl.col("num_of_delays_nas"))
    )
)

# Show the first 5 rows
print(df.select(["month", "num_of_delays_weather", "num_of_delays_late_aircraft", "num_of_delays_nas", "total_weather_delay"]).head(5))

title: Creating New Columns
text:
QUESTION 4

Using the new weather variable calculated above, create a boxplot showing the distribution of flights delayed by weather for each season. Describe what you learn from this graph.

Winter has the highest median and variability, showing that weather delays in Winter are more frequent and unpredictable during the first three months of the year. There are also several outliers in Winter and Fall, showing that severe blizzards and other weather events cause massive spikes in delays. Spring is the safest time to fly, so April through June is generally the most reliable for flight schedules.


Show the code
df_plot = (
    df.filter(pl.col("season") != "Unknown")
    .with_columns(
        pl.col("season").cast(pl.Enum(["Winter", "Spring", "Summer", "Fall"]))
    )
)

p = (
    ggplot(df_plot, aes(x='season', y='total_weather_delay', fill='season')) 
    + geom_boxplot(show_legend=False, outlier_size=1)
    + labs(
        title="Weather-Related Flight Delays by Season",
        subtitle="Includes 100% Weather, 30% Late Aircraft, and 40-65% NAS delays",
        x="Season",
        y="Flights Delayed (in 1,000s)"
    )
    + theme_minimal2()
    + scale_fill_brewer(type='qual', palette='Set1')
)

p.show()

title: Famous Names
text:
QUESTION 1

Make a new column that represents the total number of babies with your name for only those states in which you have lived. (If you have only ever lived in one state, pick at least one other state so you get practice summing across columns). Make a graph displaying that total for your name over time.
The name Sydney reached its peak in all the states IвҖҷve lived previously (CO, WA, WV, ID, UT, NE) in 2000, but it was still almost as popular in 2001, when I was born. Roughly 750 babies total in the six states IвҖҷve lived were named Sydney in 2001. Prior to 1980, the name had little to no popularity, and since its height it has dwindled off again in all six states to only around a hundred born total yearly.


Show the code
# Q1
my_name = "Sydney"
my_states = ['UT', 'WA', 'NE', 'WV', 'CO', 'ID'] 

# Filter for my name and create a column summing the states
q1_data = (df
    .filter(pl.col('name') == my_name)
    .with_columns(
        (pl.col('WA') + pl.col('CO') + pl.col('ID') + pl.col('UT') + pl.col('WV') + pl.col('NE')).alias('my_states_total')
    )
    .select(['year', 'my_states_total'])
    .group_by('year')
    .sum()
    .sort('year')
    .with_columns(pl.col('year').cast(pl.Int64))
)

# Get birth year value for reference mark
birth_year = 2001
birth_year_value = q1_data.filter(pl.col('year') == birth_year)['my_states_total'][0]

# Create chart
(
    ggplot(q1_data, aes(x='year', y='my_states_total')) +
    geom_line(color='#1f77b4', size=1) +
    geom_vline(xintercept=birth_year, linetype='dashed', color='red', size=1) +
    geom_point(x=birth_year, y=birth_year_value, color='red', size=3) +
    ggtitle(f"Usage of Name '{my_name}' in States I've lived over time") +
    xlab("Year") +
    ylab("Number of Babies Named") +
    scale_x_continuous(format='d')
)

title: Famous Names
text:
QUESTION 2

LetвҖҷs look at a name that is special for members of the church: Brigham. For each year, calculate what proportion of babies named Brigham were born in Idaho and Utah? Make a graph displaying the proportion over time.

The graph shows that in the early years of the dataset, 100% of babies named Brigham were born in Idaho and Utah, though the absolute numbers were very small during this period. This makes sense as the name Brigham (after Brigham Young, the second president of the LDS Church) was primarily used within the Mormon community concentrated in these states. As the name gained wider usage across the country, the proportion dropped significantly, reaching a low point around 1970-1980 where only about 40% of babies named Brigham were born in Idaho and Utah. In recent years, the proportion has stabilized around 45-50% born in Utah or Idaho.


Show the code
# Q2
# Filter for the name Brigham
brigham_data = (df
    .filter(pl.col('name') == 'Brigham')
    .with_columns(
        # Calculate ID + UT total
        (pl.col('ID') + pl.col('UT')).alias('id_ut_total'),
        # Calculate proportion
        ((pl.col('ID') + pl.col('UT')) / pl.col('Total')).alias('id_ut_proportion')
    )
    .select(['year', 'id_ut_proportion'])
    .group_by('year')
    .agg(pl.col('id_ut_proportion').mean())
    .sort('year')
    .with_columns(pl.col('year').cast(pl.Int64))
)

# Create chart
(
    ggplot(brigham_data, aes(x='year', y='id_ut_proportion')) +
    geom_line(color='#2ca02c', size=1) +
    geom_hline(yintercept=0.5, linetype='dashed', color='gray', size=0.8) +
    ggtitle("Proportion of Babies Named 'Brigham' Born in Idaho and Utah") +
    xlab("Year") +
    ylab("Proportion (ID + UT / Total)") +
    scale_x_continuous(format='d')
)

title: Famous Names
text:
QUESTION 3

Consider doing some data validation. For the name David, calculate a sum of all 50 states and District of Columbia for each year. Now compare that total to the value in the вҖҳTotalвҖҷ column. In which years, if any, do they not agree?

There were no years where the name DavidвҖҷs total in the Total column didnвҖҷt match the calculated totals of all states.


Show the code
# Q3
# Get all state columns (everything except name, year, and Total)
state_columns = [col for col in df.columns if col not in ['name', 'year', 'Total']]

# Filter for David and calculate state sum
david_data = (df
    .filter(pl.col('name') == 'David')
    .with_columns(
        # Sum all state columns
        pl.sum_horizontal(state_columns).alias('state_sum')
    )
    .select(['year', 'Total', 'state_sum'])
    .with_columns(
        # Calculate difference
        (pl.col('Total') - pl.col('state_sum')).alias('difference')
    )
    .sort('year')
)

# Find years where they don't match (allow small rounding differences)
mismatched_years = david_data.filter(pl.col('difference').abs() > 0.1)

# Display the mismatched years
print("Years where state sum does not equal Total column:")
print(mismatched_years)

# Create visualization of the difference over time
plot_data = david_data.with_columns(pl.col('year').cast(pl.Int64))

(
    ggplot(plot_data, aes(x='year', y='difference')) +
    geom_line(color='#d62728', size=1) +
    geom_hline(yintercept=0, linetype='dashed', color='black', size=0.8) +
    ggtitle("Difference Between Total Column and State Sum for Name 'David'") +
    xlab("Year") +
    ylab("Difference (Total - State Sum)") +
    scale_x_continuous(format='d')
)

title: WhatвҖҷs in a Name?
text:
QUESTION 1

How does your name at your birth year compare to its use historically? Your must provide a chart. The years labels on your charts should not include a comma.
Sydney was hardly used at all until around 1985 when its popularity skyrocketed. It peaked in use around 200, meaning in 2001 it started its decline from popularity, but was still one of the top three years for that name being used.


Show the code
# Filter data
my_name = "Sydney"
birth_year = 2001
name_data = (df
    .filter(pl.col('name') == my_name)
    .select(['year', 'Total'])
    .group_by('year')
    .sum()
    .sort('year')
)

# Convert 'year' to integer to avoid type mismatch
name_data = name_data.with_columns(pl.col('year').cast(pl.Int64))

# Get the value for birth year
birth_year_value = name_data.filter(pl.col('year') == birth_year)['Total'][0]

# Create line chart with Lets-Plot
(
    ggplot(name_data, aes(x='year', y='Total')) +
    geom_line(color='#1f77b4') +
    geom_vline(xintercept=birth_year, linetype='dashed', color='red', size=1) +
    geom_point(x=birth_year, y=birth_year_value, color='red', size=3) +
    ggtitle(f"Usage of Name '{my_name}' Over Time") +
    xlab("Year") +
    ylab("Number of Babies Named") +
    scale_x_continuous(format='d')
)

title: WhatвҖҷs in a Name?
text:
QUESTION 2
Think of a unique name from a famous movie. Plot the usage of that name and see how changes line up with the movie release. Does it look like the movie had an effect on usage?
I used the name Forrest from Forrest Gump. The name was actually pretty popular between 1910 and 1970, where it started to dwindle off. When the movie came out in 1994 its popularity went higher than ever before. Only a few years later though it became less popular than it had ever been, but itвҖҷs slowly been making a comeback since 2010.


Show the code
# Filter data for Forrest
forrest_data = (df
    .filter(pl.col('name') == 'Forrest')
    .select(['year', 'Total'])
    .group_by('year')
    .sum()
    .sort('year')
)

# Convert 'year' to integer
forrest_data = forrest_data.with_columns(pl.col('year').cast(pl.Int64))

# Get the value for 1994
movie_year_value = forrest_data.filter(pl.col('year') == 1994)['Total'][0]

# Create line chart
(
    ggplot(forrest_data, aes(x='year', y='Total')) +
    geom_line(color='#9467bd') +
    geom_vline(xintercept=1994, linetype='dashed', color='red', size=1) +
    geom_point(x=1994, y=movie_year_value, color='red', size=3) +
    ggtitle("Usage of Name 'Forrest' Over Time") +
    xlab("Year") +
    ylab("Number of Babies Named Forrest") +
    scale_x_continuous(format='d')
)

title: WhatвҖҷs in a Name?
text:
QUESTION 3
Mary, Martha, Peter, and Paul are all Christian names. From 1920 - 2000, compare the name usage of each of the four names in a single chart. What trends do you notice?
Before 1960, Mary was used almost 7 times more often than any of the other names, but it fell off sharply, and by 2000 was about as common as the other names. Peter and Martha have both remained relatively steady around 5000 babies named that per year, but Paul has a short period from 1945-1970 where it spiked higher than those two before it went back to around the same level as them again.


Show the code
# Filter Data
names = ['Mary', 'Martha', 'Peter', 'Paul']
name_year_data = (df
    .filter(
        (pl.col('name').is_in(names)) & 
        (pl.col('year') >= 1920) & 
        (pl.col('year') <= 2000)
    )
    .select(['name', 'year', 'Total'])
    .group_by(['name', 'year'])
    .sum()
    .sort('year')
)

# Convert 'year' to integer
name_year_data = name_year_data.with_columns(pl.col('year').cast(pl.Int64))

# Create line chart
(
    ggplot(name_year_data, aes(x='year', y='Total', color='name')) +
    geom_line(size=1) +
    geom_vline(xintercept=1960, linetype='dashed', color='black', size=1) +
    ggtitle("Usage of Names Mary, Martha, Peter, and Paul (1920-2000)") +
    xlab("Year") +
    ylab("Number of Babies Named") +
    scale_x_continuous(format='d') +
    scale_color_manual(values=['#1f77b4', '#ff7f0e', '#2ca02c', '#d62728'])
)

text:
QUESTION 1

Which airport has the worst delays? Describe the metric you chose, and why you chose it to determine the вҖңworstвҖқ airport. Your answer should include a summary table that lists (for each airport) the total number of flights, total number of delayed flights, proportion of delayed flights, and average delay time in hours.

I chose to use the proportion of delayed flights as the metric because it normalizes any differences in the total number of flights across airports, not discriminating between airports with very different flight volumes. This allows for a fair comparison of delay performance across airports.


Show the code
summary_table = (
    df.filter(
        ~pl.col("num_of_delays_total").cast(pl.Utf8).is_in(["-999", "1500+"])
    )
    .with_columns([
        pl.col("num_of_delays_total").cast(pl.Int64),
        pl.col("num_of_flights_total").cast(pl.Int64),
        pl.col("minutes_delayed_total").cast(pl.Float64)
    ])
    .group_by("airport_code")
    .agg([
        pl.col("num_of_flights_total").sum().alias("total_flights"),
        pl.col("num_of_delays_total").sum().alias("total_delayed"),
        ((pl.col("minutes_delayed_total").sum() / 60) / pl.col("num_of_delays_total").sum()).alias("avg_delay_hours")
    ])
    .with_columns(
        (pl.col("total_delayed") / pl.col("total_flights")).alias("prop_delayed")
    )
    .sort("prop_delayed", descending=True)
)

print(summary_table)

title: Data Comparison Challenge
text:
Question 1

Which delay is the worst delay? Keep working with the cleaned dataset you created in the previous task. Compare the proportion of flights delayed due to Carrier, to Security, and to Late Aircraft. Show this comparison in a Chart and a Table. Describe your results.

To determine the worst delay type, I compared the proportion of flights imppacted by three specific categories: carrier, security, and late-arriving aircraft. Based on the calculations and the bar chart, Late Aircraft delays are the most common, followed by Carrier and then Security delays.


Show the code
df_clean = df.with_columns([
    pl.when(pl.col(name).cast(pl.Utf8).is_in(["-999", "1500+", "n/a"]))
    .then(None)
    .otherwise(pl.col(name))
    .alias(name) 
    for name in df.columns
])

df_filled = df_clean.with_columns([
    pl.col("^minutes_delayed.*$").cast(pl.Float64).fill_null(pl.col("^minutes_delayed.*$").cast(pl.Float64).median()),
    pl.col("^num_of.*$").cast(pl.Float64).fill_null(pl.col("^num_of.*$").cast(pl.Float64).mean())
])

df_long = df_filled.unpivot(
    index=["airport_code", "num_of_flights_total"],
    on=["num_of_delays_carrier", "num_of_delays_late_aircraft", "num_of_delays_security"],
    variable_name="delay_type",
    value_name="delay_count"
)

comparison_table = (
    df_long.group_by("delay_type")
    .agg([
        pl.col("delay_count").sum().alias("total_delays"),
        (pl.col("delay_count").sum() / pl.col("num_of_flights_total").sum()).alias("proportion")
    ])
    .sort("proportion", descending=True)
)

print(comparison_table)

p1 = (
    ggplot(comparison_table, aes(x='delay_type', y='proportion', fill='delay_type'))
    + geom_bar(stat='identity', show_legend=False)
    + scale_y_continuous(format='.2%')
    + labs(
        title="Comparison of Flight Delay Reasons",
        x="Delay Category",
        y="Proportion of Total Flights"
    )
    + theme_minimal2()
)
p1.show()

title: Star Wars for Dummies
text:
Executive Summary
The objective of this analysis was to process survey data from FiveThirtyEight regarding Star Wars fans to help enable targeted marketing and predictive modeling. I was asked to clean the dataset, extract any meaningful information, and build a model to predict high-income respondents. The results confirm that Star Wars survey data is a reliable source for behavioral insights. My recommendations would be to use the extracted names for Star Wars Day promotions, and to focus high income marketing efforts on the demographics that are identified in the feature importance results with the highest scores.
Data Preparation: I successfully cleaned the вҖңmessyвҖқ survey dataset, extracting respondent names from email addresses and mapping categorical preferences to numeric scales for easier understanding.
Machine Learning: I developed a Random Forest model to predict if a respondent earns at least $50,000 annually. The model achieved an accuracy of approximately 59.88%, identifying age and level of education as the most significant predictors of income (followed by their opinion on Jar Jar Binks).
STRETCH CHALLENGE: Yes, the stretch challenge was completed by recreating two of the original chart visualizations from the article and showing that the data used here matches the original findings, validating the dataset.

Task 1
The raw data contained metadata before the headers, which I bypassed using find RespondentID and io.BytesIO. To assist the marketing team, I used Regex to parse the email column into first name, last name, and a standardized lowercase email domain. As required, there is a table below showing the number of times each email domain is used among the respondents.


Show the code
sw_cleaned = sw.with_columns(
    name_part = pl.col("email").str.extract(r"^([^@]+)"),
    email_domain = pl.col("email").str.extract(r"@([^.]+)").str.to_lowercase().str.strip_chars()
).with_columns(
    # FIRST NAME
    first_name = pl.when(pl.col("name_part").str.contains(r"\."))
      .then(pl.col("name_part").str.extract(r"^([^.]+)"))
      .when(pl.col("name_part").str.contains(r"_"))
      .then(pl.col("name_part").str.extract(r"_([a-zA-Z]+)"))
      .otherwise(pl.col("name_part").str.extract(r"([a-zA-Z]+)$")),
                
    # LAST NAME
    last_name = pl.when(pl.col("name_part").str.contains(r"\."))
      .then(pl.col("name_part").str.extract(r"\.([^@]+)"))
      .when(pl.col("name_part").str.contains(r"_"))
      .then(pl.col("name_part").str.extract(r"^([a-zA-Z]+)_"))
      .otherwise(pl.col("name_part").str.extract(r"^([a-zA-Z]+)"))
)

# Verify

Task 3
I finalized the data by dropping identifiers (Name, Email, RespondentID) and applying One-Hot Encoding to remaining categorical strings. Our target variable was defined as a binary indicator for Household Income \ge $50,000. Essentially this just means I dropped any non-numeric values so the model could be trained appropriately, and then told it what to predict with the data it recieves.

# 1. Create the Target Column

  target = pl.when(pl.col("income_num") >= 3).then(1).otherwise(0)
)

sw_ml_ready = sw_ml_ready.drop(cols_to_drop)

# 3. One-Hot Encode
sw_final = sw_ml_ready.to_dummies(
  columns=[col for col, dtype in sw_ml_ready.schema.items() if dtype == pl.String]
)

# 4. Handle any remaining Nulls
sw_final = sw_final.fill_null(0)

print(f"Final feature count: {len(sw_final.columns)}")

title: Star Wars for Dummies
text:
Results

Model Description
I utilized a Random Forest Classifier with 100 estimators. This model is ideal for survey data as it captures interactions between features (for example, how Age and Education combined might predict Income) without assuming a linear relationship.


Model Accuracy
The model achieved an accuracy of approximately 59.88%. This model successfully exceeds the clientвҖҷs 55% accuracy threshold. It may seem like a lower score than what is generally hoped for, but when you think about how accurately it is able to predict a personвҖҷs yearly income based off of their opinions on Star Wars films, it seems like a reasonable, and even impressive performance.


Show the code
# Separate Features
X = sw_final.drop("target")
y = sw_final.select("target")

# Split data
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
  X.to_numpy(), 
  y.to_numpy().ravel(), 
  test_size=0.2, 
  random_state=42
)
# Initialize and Train
model = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Predict and Evaluate
y_pred = model.predict(X_test)
accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)

print(f"Model Accuracy: {accuracy:.2%}")
print("\nClassification Report:")
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred))


Model Accuracy: 59.88%

Classification Report:

weighted avg       0.61      0.60      0.60       167





Feature Importance
The following chart shows which factors were the strongest predictors of a respondent making over $50k. Higher scores indicate greater importance in the modelвҖҷs decision-making process.

feature_importance_df = pl.DataFrame({
    "feature": X.columns,
    "importance": model.feature_importances_
}).sort("importance", descending=True).head(10)

  ggplot(feature_importance_df, aes(x=as_discrete('feature', order_by='importance'), y='importance')) +
  geom_bar(stat='identity', fill='#008fd5') +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title="Top 10 Predictors of Income >= $50k",
    subtitle="Key factors identifying high-income respondents",
    x="", 
    y="Importance Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
)

p_importance.show()

title: How good is it, really?
text:
QUESTION 1

Describe the quality of your classification model using 2-3 different evaluation metrics. You also need to explain how to define/interpret each of the evaluation metrics you use.

The Random Forest Model shows high accuracy and quality with three metrics: accuracy (92.4%), balanced accuracy (92%), and F1 score (93.9%). Accuracy measures the overall proportion of correct guesses. Balanced accuracy is used when the dataset might contain more of one type of housing than the other, so it shows whether the model is equally good at identifying both pre and post 1980 homes. The high F1 score means thereвҖҷs a good balance between the precision and recall, meaning it rarely misclassifies modern homes as old (and vice versa), so it is overall a reliable tool for the Colorado health departmentвҖҷs safety screenings.


Show the code
# Include and execute your code here
# Don't forget, you need another import statement to import the desired ML model from sklearn
dwellings = dwellings.with_columns(
    before1980 = pl.when(pl.col("yrbuilt") < 1980).then(1).otherwise(0)
)

X = dwellings.drop(["before1980", "yrbuilt", "parcel"], strict=False)
y = dwellings["before1980"]

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X.to_pandas(), y.to_pandas(), test_size=0.20, random_state=42
)


rf = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, max_depth=15, random_state=42, class_weight='balanced')
rf.fit(X_train, y_train)
y_pred = rf.predict(X_test)


print(f"Accuracy: {metrics.accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred):.3f}")
print(f"Balanced Accuracy: {metrics.balanced_accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred):.3f}")
print(f"F1 Score: {metrics.f1_score(y_test, y_pred):.3f}")

F1 Score: 0.939

Features like live area and one story were the most critical, shuffling them caused the biggest drop in accuracy because they represent bigger shifts in DenverвҖҷs architectural history. The three most significant features, live area, number of baths, and one story, show clear shifts that define the pre/post 1980 housing styles. text:
QUESTION 1

Join the dwellings_neighborhoods_ml.csv data to the dwelling_ml.csv on the parcel column to create a new dataset. Duplicate the code for the model you recommended in the previous taskn above and update it to use this data. Explain the differences and if this changes the model you recomend to the Client.

Merging the datasets actually improved our accuracy from 92% to 94.71%. The jump suggests that location is a good predictor of home age in Denver. Older neighborhoods often have distinct architectural styles and signatures compared to newer developments. Because the F1 score for identifying pre 1980 homes rose to 96%, I would recommend using this combined model to the client. It shows a more geographically aware screening tool thatвҖҷs less likely to miss older homes in historic districts, and is a better solution for safety assessments.


Show the code
combined_data = dwellings.join(neighborhoods, on="parcel")


combined_data = combined_data.with_columns(
    before1980 = pl.when(pl.col("yrbuilt") < 1980).then(1).otherwise(0)
)


X = combined_data.drop(["before1980", "before_1980", "yrbuilt", "parcel"], strict=False)
y = combined_data["before1980"]

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X.to_pandas(), y.to_pandas(), test_size=0.20, random_state=42
)


gbc = GradientBoostingClassifier(n_estimators=100, learning_rate=0.1, max_depth=5, random_state=42)
gbc.fit(X_train, y_train)

title: Regression Model Challenge
text:
QUESTION 2

Can you build a model that predicts the year a house was built? Note this is a regression ML model, not a classifier. Report appropriate evaluation metrics for the model. Explain the model and the evaluation metrics you used to determine if the model is good.

Mean Absolute Error hovers around 10 years, meaning on average the models predictions are only a decade away from the actual construction date. The Root Mean Squared Error is 15.37 years, which shows that while most predictions are close, there are some outliers where the model is further off, likely due to major renovations to make an old home seem newer. The R-squared score is .83, meaning the model explains 83% of the variance in DenverвҖҷs housing ages based on physical features and location. Overall, the model follows the scatter plotвҖҷs вҖңperfect predictionвҖқ line relatively closely, showing that the model is performing well.


Show the code
y_reg = combined_data["yrbuilt"]


X_train_r, X_test_r, y_train_r, y_test_r = train_test_split(
    X.to_pandas(), y_reg.to_pandas(), test_size=0.20, random_state=42
)


gbr = GradientBoostingRegressor(n_estimators=200, max_depth=5, learning_rate=0.1, random_state=42)
gbr.fit(X_train_r, y_train_r)


y_pred_r = gbr.predict(X_test_r)

mae = mean_absolute_error(y_test_r, y_pred_r)
r2 = r2_score(y_test_r, y_pred_r)
rmse = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_test_r, y_pred_r))

print(f"--- Regression Model Metrics ---")
print(f"Mean Absolute Error (MAE): {mae:.2f} years")
print(f"Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE): {rmse:.2f} years")
print(f"R-squared Score: {r2:.3f}")


results_df = pd.DataFrame({'Actual': y_test_r, 'Predicted': y_pred_r})

ggplot(results_df.sample(500), aes(x='Actual', y='Predicted')) + \
    geom_point(alpha=0.4, color='darkgreen') + \
    geom_abline(slope=1, intercept=0, color='red', linetype='dashed') + \
    labs(title="Regression: Predicted vs. Actual Year Built",

Write an SQL query that provides playerID, yearID, and batting average for players with at least 1 at bat that year. Sort the table from highest batting average to lowest, and then by playerid alphabetically. Show the top 5 results in your report.
When the minimum requirement is set to just one at-bat, the results show players who may have had a вҖңperfectвҖқ batting average in a single season but only went to bat one time that year. This can be misleading since it doesnвҖҷt reflect their overall performance over multiple seasons.

SELECT 
    playerID, 
    yearID, 
    (CAST(H AS FLOAT) / AB) AS batting_avg
FROM batting
WHERE AB >= 1
ORDER BY batting_avg DESC, playerID ASC
LIMIT 5
    """

title: Baseball SQL Query

QUESTION 3
Now modify the SQL query to calculate the batting average for players over their entire careers (all years combined). Only include players with at least 100 at bats over their entire career, and print the top 5 results.
Raising our minimum threshold to 100 at bats over a playerвҖҷs entire career helps us filter out players who may have had a few great seasons but didnвҖҷt play enough games overall to be considered truly representative. It instead shows us the most consistent performers over their entire careers, like Ty CobbвҖҷs record .366 average over his career.

q3 = """
SELECT 
    playerID, 
    SUM(H) * 1.0 / SUM(AB) AS career_ba
FROM batting
GROUP BY playerID
HAVING SUM(AB) >= 100
ORDER BY career_ba DESC, playerID ASC
LIMIT 5

QUESTION 1

Identify the top 10 players with the longest Major League Baseball careers. Include their playerID, first_name, last_name, career_length

Nick Altrock holds the longest career in this dataset at 35 years. This is a unique case in baseball history, as he had a long gap between his primary pitching years and several hitting appearances later on. Nolan Ryan appears in the top 10 as well, with a 27 year career, showing his status as one of the most durable players in the sport. Only 4 players exceed the 30 year mark, showing how rare that longevity is in professional sports.


Show the code
people = pl.read_database("SELECT playerID, nameFirst, nameLast, debut, finalGame FROM people", con)

longest_careers = (
    people
    .with_columns([
        pl.col("debut").str.to_date("%Y-%m-%d", strict=False).dt.year().alias("start_year"),
        pl.col("finalGame").str.to_date("%Y-%m-%d", strict=False).dt.year().alias("end_year")
    ])
    .with_columns(
        (pl.col("end_year") - pl.col("start_year")).alias("career_length")
    )
    .filter(pl.col("career_length").is_not_null())
    .sort("career_length", descending=True)
    .select([
        pl.col("playerID"),
        pl.col("nameFirst").alias("first_name"),
        pl.col("nameLast").alias("last_name"),
        pl.col("career_length")
    ])
    .head(10)
)

print(longest_careers)

title: Baseball SQL Challenge
text:
QUESTION 2

Write an SQL query that pulls the tables you need into a polars dataframe. Then, use python polars to create a summary table showing the average salary for each position (e.g., pitcher, catcher, outfielder). Position information can be found in the fielding table in the POS column. Include the following columns: position, average_salary, total_players, highest_salary. The highest_salary column should display the highest salary ever earned by a player in that position. Additionally, add a new column to the summary table you just created to label the average salary: if the average salary is above $3 million, categorize it as вҖңHigh SalaryвҖқ; if the average salary is between $2 million and $3 million, categorize it as вҖңMedium SalaryвҖқ; otherwise, categorize it as вҖңLow Salary.вҖқ Order the table by average salary in descending order.

The вҖңHigh SalaryвҖқ position is first base, which stands out as the highest paid position on average at roughly $3.3 million. While pitchers have one of the highest individual salaries ever earned ($33M), their average salary is only $1.9M due to the volume of players in that position. Catchers have one of the lowest average salaries at $1.4M due to their iffensive production being lower than infielders or outfielders, and it usually has a high physical toll on them leading to shorter careers. The вҖңMedium SalaryвҖқ bracket includes mainly outfielders, third basemen, and shortstops, all averaging between $2M-$3M.


Show the code
fielding = pl.read_database("SELECT playerID, yearID, POS, G FROM fielding", con)
salaries = pl.read_database("SELECT playerID, yearID, salary FROM salaries", con)


primary_positions = (
    fielding
    .sort("G", descending=True)
    .group_by(["playerID", "yearID"])
    .first() 
)


salary_summary = (
    salaries
    .join(primary_positions, on=["playerID", "yearID"], how="inner")
    .group_by("POS")
    .agg([
        pl.col("salary").mean().alias("average_salary"),
        pl.col("playerID").n_unique().alias("total_players"),
        pl.col("salary").max().alias("highest_salary")
    ])

    .with_columns(
        pl.when(pl.col("average_salary") > 3_000_000)
        .then(pl.lit("High Salary"))
        .when(pl.col("average_salary").is_between(2_000_000, 3_000_000))
        .then(pl.lit("Medium Salary"))
        .otherwise(pl.lit("Low Salary"))
        .alias("salary_category")
    )
    .sort("average_salary", descending=True)
    .rename({"POS": "position"})
)

print(salary_summary)
